transcript:
What you're watching is a field of phrases radiating out from the center, some upright, some rotated on their side — and one by one, a word starts flickering red and starts eating itself from both ends inward.

That corrosion is deliberate: look at "progress scraping," where characters dissolve "from edges inward, meeting in the middle" — the word doesn't fade uniformly, it gets pincered. And the palette up top isn't just colors, it's a small elegy: "still living" for cream text not yet touched, "being scraped" for that violent red flash, "become ghost" for the faint blue-gray remainder. Once a word fully corrupts, it doesn't vanish — it gets copied into a separate ghost group, permanently dimmed, still sitting there in the dark.

So the piece never actually ends. When every word has been consumed, the code quietly resets — not to erase the ghosts, but to let new words start dissolving on top of that same faint residue. It's built to be haunted by its own history, on a loop, forever almost-erased.
